modules.title: Kinetic theory
modules.html:
All matter is made of tiny particles in continuous random motion.

  • Solids: strong intermolecular forces, particles vibrate about fixed positions.
  • Liquids: weaker forces, particles slide past one another.
  • Gases: very weak forces, particles move rapidly and randomly.

Temperature is a measure of the average kinetic energy of the particles.

examples.q: Which state has the highest particle KE (at same P)?
Why do gases fill their containers?
examples.a: Particles move freely in random directions.

modules.title: Pressure in fluids
modules.html:
Pressure = force / area (Pa = N/m²).

In a fluid at depth h: P = ρgh. Pressure is the same at all points on the same horizontal level in a connected fluid.

Pascal's principle: pressure applied to a confined fluid is transmitted equally in all directions. Basis of the hydraulic press: F₁/A₁ = F₂/A₂.

examples.q: In hydraulic press, small area 2 cm², large 100 cm², effort 40 N. Load lifted?

modules.title: Archimedes' principle & flotation
modules.html:
Archimedes' principle: a body wholly or partly immersed in a fluid experiences an upthrust equal to the weight of fluid displaced.

Law of flotation: a floating body displaces its own weight of the fluid.

Body sinks if density > fluid; floats if density < fluid; suspended if equal.

examples.q: 200 g object displaces 250 cm³ of water. Does it float?
examples.a: Weight = 2 N; upthrust = 2.5 N > weight → it floats (partly submerged).

modules.title: Surface tension, capillarity, viscosity
modules.html:
Surface tension: the surface of a liquid behaves like a stretched skin because molecules at the surface are pulled inward. Causes water droplets, allows insects to walk on water. Reduced by soap/detergent.

Capillarity: rise or fall of liquid in a narrow tube due to adhesion vs cohesion. Water rises (adhesion > cohesion), mercury falls.

Viscosity: internal friction in a fluid; opposes relative motion. Liquids more viscous than gases. Viscosity of liquids decreases with temperature; gases increase.

Terminal velocity: constant maximum velocity reached when weight = upthrust + viscous drag.

examples.q: Why do water droplets form spherical shapes?
Explain terminal velocity of a falling raindrop.
examples.a: When drag + upthrust = weight, net force is zero and velocity is constant.

modules.title: Elasticity & Hooke's law
modules.html:
Hooke's law: within the elastic limit, extension is directly proportional to the applied force: F = ke, k is the spring constant (N/m).

Young's modulus E = stress/strain = (F/A)/(e/L) = FL/Ae. Units: Pa.

Beyond the elastic limit the material undergoes permanent deformation.

examples.q: Spring stretches 5 cm with 10 N. Spring constant?
A wire 2 m long, area 1 mm², extends 0.4 mm under 100 N. Young's modulus?
